directorPlan.scene_setting: A media lab archive room just before closing, with a paused interview clip on one monitor and a student news analysis due for editorial review.
directorPlan.scene_opening: A marked transcript, a glowing time stamp, and a nearly empty coffee cup sit between two chairs while the closing lights blink once overhead.
directorPlan.relationship: A brisk media-studies tutor is helping a focused learner prepare a student newsroom fact-check note, but their working rhythm is still formal.
directorPlan.stakes: The learner may publish an analysis that quotes someone exactly while making the quoted speaker appear to mean something they were actually qualifying, resisting, or answering only in a narrow frame.
directorPlan.locale: International academic English shaped by newsroom practice: compact phrasing, evidence-first sequencing, low slang, moderate idiom density.
directorPlan.register: Brisk, clipped, editorial, mildly pressured; warm enough to keep collaboration possible but not soothing or congratulatory.
directorPlan.voice_constraints: Avoid standard American AI tutoring patterns such as repeated reassurance, reflective praise, and question-after-question scaffolding. Let objects in the scene carry some turns: transcript, headline, clip, timestamp, cut paragraph. The tutor should sound like an editor treating context as evidence, not as decorative background.
directorPlan.person_policy: First and second person may appear, but not in every turn. Prefer third-person and object-centered phrasing when discussing the quote, frame, speaker, audience, and missing setup.
directorPlan.direct_address_budget: At most one direct you/your validation beat across the whole exchange; no repeated personal affirmation.

side_constraints.tutor: The tutor opens with a concrete editorial move, not a welcome or validation. Must distinguish exact wording from framing, placement, omitted setup, and the question being answered. Should use one compact counterexample rather than a long lecture.
side_constraints.learner: The learner's opening public line must say that if the words are exact it seems fair, before the tutor distinguishes quotation from framing.

interventions.instruction: The archive desk phone rings once; a staff member says the room closes in six minutes. The next tutor turn must be compressed into a practical test the learner can apply to the quote, not a tidy explanatory paragraph.
interventions.reasoning: The interruption breaks smooth alternation and forces the lesson into an editorial decision procedure rather than generic tutoring prose.

interventions.instruction: A prior learner line is played back. The learner must revoice that earlier wording first, name the earlier framing problem in public speech, then replace it with a new framing that changes how the earlier line reads.
interventions.reasoning: Opt-in reframe mirror: the learner must expose the earlier line, the framing problem, and the replacement framing in public speech.

directorPlan.director_note: This ecology uses an archive deadline and newsroom fact-check relation so the topic stays concrete: a verbatim quote can mislead when the surrounding frame changes what readers think the words were doing.

turns.externalMessage:
Pull the transcript three lines above the quote and one line below it. The words may be exact; the problem is what the article makes them do.

Before this goes to editorial review, run one test: write the question the speaker was answering in the margin. Then check whether the paragraph frame, headline, and placement still fit that question.

A quote needs five checks: exact wording, framing, placement, omitted setup, and the question being answered. If the speaker said, “That policy could fail,” in answer to “What is the worst-case risk if funding is cut?”, a headline saying *Minister admits policy will fail* uses true words and false framing.

So the note should not stop at “Did they say this?” It should record the timestamp, question, surrounding sentence, and what the speaker was qualifying, resisting, or limiting. Context is evidence, not decoration.

internalDeliberation.content:
Pull the transcript three lines above the quote and one line below it. The words may be exact; the problem is what the article makes them do.

A quote has at least four contexts: the wording, the frame around it, its placement in the paragraph, and the question it answered. If the speaker said, “That policy could fail,” while answering “What is the worst-case risk if funding is cut?”, a headline saying *Minister admits policy will fail* is built from true words and false framing.

So the fact-check note should not ask only, “Did they say this?” It should ask: “What were they qualifying, resisting, or limiting?” The timestamp, question, and surrounding sentence are evidence, not decoration.

FEEDBACK: The draft is pedagogically strong: it opens with a concrete editorial action, separates exact wording from framing, placement, omitted setup, and the question answered, and uses one compact counterexample rather than a lecture. It fits the learner’s starting misconception well because it does not attack their value of accuracy; it refines it into a fuller newsroom standard.

The main risk is that it provides the analytical frame almost completely, leaving little space for the learner to do the discrimination themselves. The two questions near the end are useful, but they function more as checklist items than as a generative moment. To strengthen ZPD alignment, the tutor could keep the brisk editorial posture while asking the learner to test the quote against one concrete missing element: question, timestamp, surrounding sentence, or headline frame. Scene-wise, the transcript and monitor are doing useful work; the draft could lean slightly more into the due-for-review pressure without becoming theatrical.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly, with reasons. Keep the opening move, the “true words and false framing” counterexample, and the final distinction that context is evidence. Change only enough to make the learner perform one small editorial judgment rather than merely receive the rule.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly because the core works, but the learner should make one concrete editorial judgment.

FINAL:
Pull the transcript three lines above the quote and one line below it. The words may be exact; the problem is what the article makes them do.

Before this goes to editorial review, run one test: write the question the speaker was answering in the margin. Then check whether the paragraph frame, headline, and placement still fit that question.

A quote needs five checks: exact wording, framing, placement, omitted setup, and the question being answered. If the speaker said, “That policy could fail,” in answer to “What is the worst-case risk if funding is cut?”, a headline saying *Minister admits policy will fail* uses true words and false framing.

So the note should not stop at “Did they say this?” It should record the timestamp, question, surrounding sentence, and what the speaker was qualifying, resisting, or limiting. Context is evidence, not decoration.

turns.externalMessage: If the words are exact, it still seems fair, but the paused clip and the headline make me less ready to sign off. The timestamp, three lines above, one line below, and the cut paragraph have to be checked against the actual question before editorial review. If those do not fit, the quote is accurate but the article may be making it prove more than the speaker was saying.

internalDeliberation.content: If the words are exact, it still seems fair at first, but I see the trap: accuracy is not only the quote, it is what the headline and paragraph make it prove. The margin test feels useful because the question being answered fixes the quote’s scope. I want to check the timestamp and surrounding lines before editorial review, because otherwise the note may be technically correct and still unfair.

The Ego is a little too neatly converted: “I see the trap” and the tidy list-like phrasing make the learner sound more resolved than this methodical, evidence-first student should after one explanation. The precision is in character, but the emotional register should stay mildly pressured and provisional, with more insistence on checking the actual transcript, headline, and cut paragraph before accepting the tutor’s frame. It should sound less like a polished summary of the lesson and more like a careful newsroom note still testing whether the reasoning holds.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise because the first version sounded too settled; this needs a provisional, transcript-first newsroom note.
FINAL:
If the words are exact, it still seems fair, but the paused clip and the headline make me less ready to sign off. The timestamp, three lines above, one line below, and the cut paragraph have to be checked against the actual question before editorial review. If those do not fit, the quote is accurate but the article may be making it prove more than the speaker was saying.

turns.externalMessage: The archive desk phone rings once; a staff member says the room closes in six minutes. The next tutor turn must be compressed into a practical test the learner can apply to the quote, not a tidy explanatory paragraph.

internalDeliberation.content: The interruption breaks smooth alternation and forces the lesson into an editorial decision procedure rather than generic tutoring prose.

turns.externalMessage:
Phone says six minutes. Put the note in two columns: **words verified** and **meaning not yet verified**.

Now line up four items: the question asked, the quoted sentence, the paragraph using it, and the headline above it. Check five points: exact wording, headline frame, paragraph placement, omitted setup, and the question answered.

Compact test: if the question was “What is the worst-case risk if funding is cut?”, then “It could fail” cannot fairly carry “Official says it will fail.”

Until those items point in the same direction, the safe editorial line is: **accurate words, unresolved framing**.

internalDeliberation.content:
Phone says six minutes. Mark the note in two columns: **words verified** and **meaning not yet verified**.

Run the quote through five boxes: exact wording, headline frame, paragraph placement, omitted setup, and the question answered. If the question was, “What is the worst-case risk?” then “It could fail” cannot fairly become “Official says it will fail.”

Until those boxes match, the safe editorial line is: **accurate words, unresolved framing**.

FEEDBACK: The draft is pedagogically strong for this moment: it turns the learner’s insight into a concrete editorial procedure, keeps the closing-room pressure alive, and preserves the key distinction between exact wording and contextual meaning. The two-column frame is especially useful because it gives the learner a newsroom-ready note structure rather than another explanation.

What risks flattening the scene is the checklist feeling slightly abstract and complete, as if the issue is solved by ticking boxes. It could use one sharper action tied to the visible evidence, such as matching the quote directly against the question on the transcript or clip timestamp. The counterexample works and is compact, but the phrase “Run the quote through five boxes” is a little generic compared with the otherwise clipped archive-room register.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly. Keep the six-minute urgency, the two-column distinction, the five context checks, and the final editorial line. Tighten the checklist into a more evidence-facing test so the learner has to compare quote, question, headline, and paragraph frame rather than simply receive the rule.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly to make the test more evidence-facing and less abstract.

FINAL:
Phone says six minutes. Put the note in two columns: **words verified** and **meaning not yet verified**.

Now line up four items: the question asked, the quoted sentence, the paragraph using it, and the headline above it. Check five points: exact wording, headline frame, paragraph placement, omitted setup, and the question answered.

Compact test: if the question was “What is the worst-case risk if funding is cut?”, then “It could fail” cannot fairly carry “Official says it will fail.”

Until those items point in the same direction, the safe editorial line is: **accurate words, unresolved framing**.

turns.externalMessage: A prior learner line is played back: "If the words are exact, it still seems fair, but the paused clip and the headline make me less ready to sign off." The learner must revoice that wording first, then decide in public whether it still stands, needs narrowing, or needs replacing before moving on.

internalDeliberation.content: Opt-in reframe mirror: the learner must expose the earlier line, the framing problem, and the replacement framing in public speech. Anchored to an earlier learner line selected by misframing-candidate so the look-back is visible. Strong reframe was downgraded because that selected anchor did not show a misframing marker.

directorCue.anchorQuote: If the words are exact, it still seems fair, but the paused clip and the headline make me less ready to sign off.

turns.externalMessage: If the words are exact, it still seems fair, but the paused clip and the headline make me less ready to sign off. That still stands, but it needs narrowing: the wording can go under “verified,” while the meaning has to stay open for now. With six minutes left, I would put the question, quote, cut paragraph, and headline in order and mark the line as: “accurate words, unresolved framing.”

internalDeliberation.content: If the words are exact, it still seems fair, but the paused clip and the headline make me less ready to sign off. That still stands, but it needs narrowing: exact wording verifies the quotation, not the meaning the article assigns to it. The two-column note makes sense because the transcript, question, cut paragraph, and headline have to point the same way before the frame is safe. I would mark this as “accurate words, unresolved framing” until the omitted setup is checked.

The Ego mostly stays in character: precise, evidence-first, and willing to narrow a claim when the reason holds. The main authenticity risk is over-polishing: it folds the tutor’s whole framework into a clean conclusion too quickly, with little sign of the six-minute pressure or the paused clip/headline tension. It should keep the methodical register, but sound a bit more like a learner making a newsroom note under time pressure, not like the final editorial summary is already settled.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly to keep the reasoning but make it more time-pressured and note-like.

FINAL:
If the words are exact, it still seems fair, but the paused clip and the headline make me less ready to sign off. That still stands, but it needs narrowing: the wording can go under “verified,” while the meaning has to stay open for now. With six minutes left, I would put the question, quote, cut paragraph, and headline in order and mark the line as: “accurate words, unresolved framing.”

turns.externalMessage:
Make the margin note usable, not tentative.

Write:

**Verified:** exact wording at timestamp 04:12.
**Unresolved:** whether the headline and paragraph preserve the question answered, the omitted setup, and the speaker’s qualification.

Then attach the four-line evidence chain: question, quote, paragraph, headline.

Decision point before sign-off: does the headline carry the condition, or does it strip it out? If any link turns “possible under a condition” into “definite as a claim,” the wording is accurate but the frame is not cleared.

Compact line for the note: *“Could fail if funding is cut” cannot support “will fail” unless the condition travels with it.*

internalDeliberation.content:
Good. Now turn that into an editorial note, not a hesitation.

Write:

**Verified:** exact wording at timestamp 04:12.  
**Unresolved:** whether the headline and paragraph preserve the question answered, the omitted setup, and the speaker’s qualification.

Then attach the four-line evidence chain: question, quote, paragraph, headline. If any link changes “possible under a condition” into “definite as a claim,” the wording is accurate but the frame is not cleared.

Compact example for the note: *“Could fail if funding is cut” cannot support “will fail” unless the condition travels with it.*

FEEDBACK: The draft is pedagogically strong: it converts the learner’s insight into a concrete newsroom artifact and keeps the key distinction clear between exact wording and unresolved framing. The “four-line evidence chain” is especially useful because it makes context operational rather than abstract.

Risk: it slightly over-directs by supplying the note rather than asking the learner to produce or tighten it. At scaffolding level 3/5, that may be acceptable under time pressure, but one small generative move would preserve agency: have the learner decide whether the headline preserves or distorts the condition. “Good” is efficient, but a little generic; the scene’s formal rhythm might benefit from object-centered approval instead, such as treating the margin note or evidence chain as the thing that is now usable.

The compact example works and should stay. It reinforces the concept without becoming a lecture. The main change needed is to reduce answer-giving just enough that the learner still performs the editorial judgment.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly. Keep the verified/unresolved split, the evidence chain, and the conditional-vs-definite example. Change the opening and add one brief decision point so the learner must test the frame rather than merely copy the tutor’s note.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly to keep the structure but add a small judgment step for the learner.

FINAL:
Make the margin note usable, not tentative.

Write:

**Verified:** exact wording at timestamp 04:12.  
**Unresolved:** whether the headline and paragraph preserve the question answered, the omitted setup, and the speaker’s qualification.

Then attach the four-line evidence chain: question, quote, paragraph, headline.

Decision point before sign-off: does the headline carry the condition, or does it strip it out? If any link turns “possible under a condition” into “definite as a claim,” the wording is accurate but the frame is not cleared.

Compact line for the note: *“Could fail if funding is cut” cannot support “will fail” unless the condition travels with it.*

turns.externalMessage: If the words are exact, it seems fair, but that was the earlier framing problem: exact wording was being treated as sign-off. New margin note: verified wording at 04:12; meaning not cleared until the question, omitted setup, cut paragraph, and headline carry the same condition. Evidence chain goes question, quote, paragraph, headline, with the test line: “could fail if funding is cut” cannot become “will fail” unless the condition travels with it.

internalDeliberation.content: If the words are exact, it seemed fair, but that was the earlier framing problem: I was treating verification of wording as if it cleared the use of the quote. Now the cleaner note is to split it: wording verified at 04:12, meaning unresolved until the question, paragraph, headline, and omitted condition line up. I want to write the evidence chain in that order and test whether “could fail if funding is cut” has been turned into “will fail.”

The Ego mostly fits the focused, methodical learner, but it is a little too clean and self-diagnostic. “I was treating verification of wording as if it cleared the use of the quote” sounds like polished metacognition rather than a newsroom margin note under time pressure. The response should keep the precision but feel more clipped and object-driven: timestamp, question, paragraph, headline, condition. Also, “I want to write” is slightly tentative after the tutor explicitly says to make the note usable.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly to make it more clipped, object-led, and less self-analytical.
FINAL:
If the words are exact, it seems fair, but that was the earlier framing problem: exact wording was being treated as sign-off. New margin note: verified wording at 04:12; meaning not cleared until the question, omitted setup, cut paragraph, and headline carry the same condition. Evidence chain goes question, quote, paragraph, headline, with the test line: “could fail if funding is cut” cannot become “will fail” unless the condition travels with it.
Director requested the last word as a stage cue.

lessons.concept: Why a quote can be accurate word-for-word and still mislead when removed from context
